blurb:
Hendrix is two years old, 63 pounds, and by all accounts kind of a goofball. The shelter describes him as a goofy boy who loves to play, and they're not overselling it. He's a pit bull mix with moderate to high energy, which means he's not bouncing off the walls but he is going to need real exercise, not just a lap around the block.

He does play rough with other dogs, so he's not right for every household. If you've got a dog who can hold their own and likes to wrestle, this is probably a great match. If you've got a timid dog or small kids, it's worth asking the shelter more before you visit. He's a sweet dog; he's just got some muscle behind his enthusiasm.

Hendrix tested positive for heartworms, but Lewisville Animal Services is handling treatment before he gets adopted out. He's also vaccinated, neutered, microchipped, and flea-treated. Come see him during visiting hours at the shelter on College Pkwy. Call the number below if you have questions about his heartworm protocol before you make the trip.
scoring_notes:
• Shelter-paid heartworm treatment removes the biggest adoption barrier while the goofy personality gives readers an easy emotional entry point
• Rough play style with dogs and caution around small kids meaningfully narrows the fit, but the honest framing builds trust with prospective adopters
• Heartworm diagnosis combined with selective placement criteria points to a prolonged shelter stay, supporting a strong urgency narrative

blurb:
Squash is a two-and-a-half-year-old boxer mix who weighs in at 57 pounds and runs at a moderate pace through life. Not a couch dog, not a chaos machine. Somewhere in the middle, which honestly is the sweet spot for a lot of households. He's good with other dogs and the shelter lists his activity level as moderate, so he's probably going to be happy with a good daily walk and some yard time rather than demanding a triathlete.

The honest gaps: housetraining status is unknown and his history with kids is also unknown. That's not a red flag, it just means he came in without much backstory. Worth asking the staff what they've observed since he's been there. They usually know more than the listing shows.

Squash is vaccinated, neutered, microchipped, heartworm negative, and flea-treated. He's ready to go. Stop by Lewisville Animal Services on College Pkwy during visiting hours to meet him in person.
scoring_notes:
• Moderate energy and dog-friendly temperament hit the sweet spot for the widest range of adult households, making him broadly marketable
• Unknown housetraining and kid history create mild hesitation but the transparent framing invites direct shelter engagement rather than discouraging interest
• Lower listing ID relative to other candidates suggests he has been in the system longer than most, giving editors a quiet urgency angle to work with

blurb:
His name in the system is Lefty, but the shelter apparently couldn't resist calling him Omen in his bio because this big shepherd mix has nothing but a bright future ahead of him. He's one year and four months old, weighs about 80 pounds, and has the energy to match. High activity level, already housetrained, and good with other dogs once he gets a proper introduction. He's the kind of dog who needs a household that's actually going to use him.

He does have a heartworm positive diagnosis, which sounds scarier than it is. Lewisville Animal Services is covering his full treatment before he goes home, so you're not inheriting a vet bill. You're just inheriting a dog who needed a little extra time before he was ready. Call the shelter at the number below if you want to talk through what the treatment timeline looks like.

Lefty is vaccinated, microchipped, neutered, and flea-treated. Walk-ins are welcome during visiting hours at the shelter on College Pkwy in Lewisville. If you've got the space and the schedule for a high-energy dog, he's worth going to meet.
scoring_notes:
• Shelter-covered heartworm treatment reframes a medical flag as a compelling act of institutional care, turning a barrier into a hook
• The name tension between 'Lefty' and 'Omen,' combined with the bright future framing, gives newsletter editors a ready-made headline angle
• Heartworm treatment timeline and high-energy profile strongly imply an extended shelter stay, making this the highest-urgency placement in the set

blurb:
Rudy is a year and a half old and still figuring out that people can be pretty great. He hasn't quite come around to petting and affection yet, but he is absolutely sold on playtime. If you want a cat who's going to go full chaos mode with a wand toy and then peace out to his own corner of the couch, Rudy is your guy.

He does best with someone who will give him space and let him warm up on his own schedule. He's not a lap cat yet, and he may not ever be, but cats like Rudy tend to bond hard once they trust you. He gets along with other cats, so a calm, confident feline roommate could actually help him settle in faster. He's not recommended for homes with kids right now.

Rudy is 10 pounds, fully vaccinated, neutered, and microchipped. He's at Lewisville Animal Services on College Pkwy. Walk in during visiting hours to say hi (from a respectable distance, at first).
shelter_hours: Visit during shelter visiting hours (confirm current hours by phone)
scoring_notes:
• The 'trust-first, bond-hard' narrative speaks directly to patient adopters and creates a meaningful long-term connection story
• No-kids restriction and need for a confident feline roommate meaningfully limits the adopter pool and extends shelter stay risk
• Behavioral profile and restricted placement criteria suggest he has been passed over repeatedly, adding quiet urgency to his feature

blurb:
Luna has lived with just about everyone: kids, adults, seniors, dogs, other cats. She's been around, she's adaptable, and at one year old she's got a lot of good years ahead. She took a little while to settle in at the shelter, which makes total sense. Shelters are loud and strange, and she's independent enough to have her own feelings about it. That's not a flaw, that's just a cat with a personality.

She's litter trained, gets along with other animals, and has real experience with kids, so she fits into a pretty wide range of households. At eight pounds, she's a smaller cat, fully vaccinated, spayed, and microchipped.

Luна is at Lewisville Animal Services on College Pkwy. Walk in during visiting hours to meet her.
shelter_hours: Visit during shelter visiting hours (confirm current hours by phone)
scoring_notes:
• Broadest household compatibility of any candidate — kids, dogs, other cats, seniors — maximizes reader match potential
• Backstory is thin and straightforward, limiting emotional hook for newsletter readers seeking a compelling narrative
• Young age and easy profile suggest a relatively short shelter stay, reducing urgency but not overall newsletter value

blurb:
Eight years of the outdoor life, and Pacino has decided he's done with it. He's ready to trade the alley for a couch, and honestly, who can blame him. He came in as a street cat with the kind of weathered look that makes you think he has stories, but the volunteers say he's a total softie underneath all that. Gentle meow, loves attention, and apparently very open to being spoiled.

He's fully vaccinated, neutered, microchipped, and ready to go. They don't know yet how he does with other pets or kids since his background is mostly a mystery, but the shelter can work with you on a meet and greet before you commit. For the right person, this is a cat who's been through a lot and just wants somewhere quiet and warm to land.

If you want to meet Pacino, head over to Lewisville Animal Services on College Pkwy during visiting hours. No appointment needed, just show up.
shelter_hours: Visit during shelter visiting hours (confirm current hours by phone)
scoring_notes:
• Weathered street cat turned softie is a deeply relatable redemption arc with strong reader appeal
• Eight years of outdoor life with unknown pet/kid compatibility narrows the pool but the meet-and-greet option reduces adoption friction
• Age and street background strongly imply a long road to this moment, signaling real urgency for a senior placement
